children.value: is the simplest atom for which the Schrödinger equation can be solved exactly. In contrast, helium, though it has only one additional electron, cannot be solved exactly due to the complexity introduced by electron-electron interactions.
Solving the Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom leverages the fact that the
Coulomb potential from the nucleus is isotropic
: it is radially symmetric and depends solely on the distance from the nucleus. This
symmetry leads to degeneracies in the energy levels
While the resulting energy eigenfunctions (atomic orbitals) are not necessarily isotropic, their dependence on angular coordinates arises fundamentally from the isotropy of the underlying potential.
Because angular momentum is conserved, energy eigenvalues can be classified by two angular momentum quantum numbers,

children.html: lll
mmm

children.value: (both integers), which quantize the magnitude and projection of angular momentum.
derived for hydrogen are also foundational in describing the structure of multi-electron atoms and molecules.
Consider one electron and one nucleus with charge

children.value: We can reduce the two-body problem to a one-body problem of an electron with reduced mass moving with respect to a fixed nucleus. Since the electron is much lighter compared to the nucleus, we can use the electron mass instead of the reduced mass,

children.html: μ≈me\mu \approx m_eμ≈me​

children.value: We have a problem of one particle moving in a symmetric potential field in 3D. We expect to get 3 quantum numbers and anticipate some degeneracies due to this radial symmetry.
in 3D is the same as for the particle in a box in 3D:

children.value: H-atom in the spherical coordinate system

children.label: H-atom in the spherical coordinate system

children.value: The Schrödinger equation for the H atom is a problem in full three dimensions with kinetic and potential energy terms. We also expect to get infinitely many eigenfunctions and eigenvalues since we have a bound electron.

children.value: We find that the electron is moving in an effective potential generated by the attractive Coulomb interaction and the repulsive orbital kinetic energy.

children.value: Full quantum solution of the H-atom

children.label: Full quantum solution of the H-atom

children.value: The complete solution of the H-atom problem is provided by writing down all eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the Hamiltonian:

children.html: H^∣n,l,ml⟩=En∣n,l,ml⟩\hat{H} |n, l, m_l\rangle = E_n |n, l, m_l\rangleH^∣n,l,ml​⟩=En​∣n,l,ml​⟩

children.value: We notice that while the wavefunction depends on three quantum numbers (coming from quantization of the radial and angular coordinates), the energy depends only on the principal quantum number
